original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded loop DoS via attacker-inflated staker list impacting any staker removal
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The rollup maintains `_stakerList` and `_stakerMap` and uses swap-and-pop deletion for removals, but there is no explicit cap on staker count. If validator whitelist is disabled (or becomes disabled), an attacker can create many stakers and bloat `_stakerList`. While core removals are O(1), any offchain or onchain logic that iterates stakers (not shown here) becomes expensive. Even within provided code, `getStakerAddress(uint64)` exposes the list; potential future/admin logic may iterate and become unusable, creating operational DoS. This hypothesis focuses on the presence of an ever-growing staker set without pruning constraints.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a common production failure mode: attacker uses permissionless entry to create large participant sets, making maintenance, monitoring, or any iteration-based function unusable. Even if current functions are O(1), the contract exposes the staker list publicly and other modules (not in provided scope) may rely on bounded staker sets.
deployment_assumptions: validatorWhitelistDisabled becomes true (via AFK/fork functions or initial config)
There exists (in full system) any onchain pathway iterating over stakers or using stakerCount() in loops (admin logic, reader, or external tooling affecting critical ops)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: RollupUserLogic.sol exposes removeWhitelistAfterFork()/removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() which can set validatorWhitelistDisabled=true; no staker cap visible in provided code.

proof_of_signal.lines: createNewStake(): pushes to _stakerList; no cap/pruning besides per-staker withdrawal; stakerCount() returns _stakerList.length
proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded participant set growth + public indexing API

preconditions: Attacker can pass onlyValidator (whitelist disabled or attacker is validator)
Attacker has capital to create many staker entries
At least one critical pathway in the full protocol/tooling iterates over stakers (to be checked)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: RollupCore.sol createNewStake() and public getters over _stakerList
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ecosystem components may rely on bounded staker set; chain can be spammed with staker entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no stake-entry throttling besides stake amount; no cap

analysis_questions: Do any in-scope or adjacent contracts (admin logic, validators utils, readers) iterate over stakers onchain?
Are there protocol-critical calls that become infeasible when stakerCount is large?
Is there any economic deterrent sufficient to prevent staker-set bloat under current stake token economics?
validation_requirements: Search codebase for loops over stakerCount/getStakerAddress
Benchmark gas for any such looped functions under large stakerCount
Model attacker cost vs operational impact

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After whitelist is disabled, attacker creates thousands of staker entries (each with minimum required stake) to bloat the staker set. Any onchain iteration-based maintenance/cleanup (if present elsewhere) becomes uncallable, stalling operational processes.
prerequisites: Significant capital to open many stakes

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but material via protocol liveness/maintenance failures
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All participants if key functions become uncallable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would likely require upgrade or admin intervention; otherwise persists

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing stress tests for large stakerCount and any iteration-based functions

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N * baseStake for N fake stakers
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor stakerCount growth and correlation with failing txs due to out-of-gas

validation_result.impact_summary: No actual code defect exists in the provided contracts. All staker list operations are O(1), and no onchain iteration over the staker list exists in scope.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that an unbounded _stakerList could cause DoS. However, careful analysis of all provided code reveals that every operation on _stakerList is O(1): createNewStake uses push, deleteStaker uses swap-and-pop, getStakerAddress uses direct index access, and stakerCount returns .length. There is no loop anywhere in the provided codebase that iterates over _stakerList or uses stakerCount() as a loop bound. The hypothesis itself acknowledges this ('While core removals are O(1)') and instead speculates about future/admin logic or external tooling that might iterate. This is not a concrete code defect. Furthermore, creating each staker requires: (1) passing onlyValidator (whitelist must be disabled), (2) depositing at least baseStake tokens, and (3) creating a valid assertion. The config validation scripts (common.ts) enforce stakeAmt >= 1 ETH, making mass staker creation economically expensive. The hypothesis's second deployment assumption - 'There exists (in full system) any onchain pathway iterating over stakers' - is not satisfied by any code in scope.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: All staker list operations confirmed O(1). No iteration exists in any provided contract over _stakerList. The hypothesis is based on speculative future code, not actual code defects.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: There is no vulnerable code path. createNewStake (RollupCore.sol) adds to _stakerList with push (O(1)). deleteStaker (RollupCore.sol) removes with swap-and-pop (O(1)). getStakerAddress (RollupCore.sol) accesses by index (O(1)). stakerCount (RollupCore.sol) returns _stakerList.length (O(1)). No function in any provided contract iterates over _stakerList.

deployment_assumptions_checked: validatorWhitelistDisabled becomes true (via AFK/fork functions or initial config)
There exists (in full system) any onchain pathway iterating over stakers or using stakerCount() in loops
config_validation.deployment_evidence: common.ts validates stakeAmt >= 1 ETH (parseEther('1')), providing significant economic barrier. The disableValidatorWhitelist config field exists but is not shown to be true by default. No deployment scripts demonstrate any iteration-based staker logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires both whitelist disabled AND existence of iteration-based onchain logic. While whitelist can theoretically be disabled, no iteration-based logic exists in any provided contract. Even if an attacker created thousands of stakers after whitelist removal, all onchain operations remain O(1). The economic cost would be N * baseStake (>= N ETH based on deployment config), and each staker must also create a valid assertion. The scenario describes a theoretical concern, not an exploitable code defect.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. All operations on the staker list in the provided code are O(1). The hypothesis is speculative about code that does not exist in scope. The getStakerAddress and stakerCount functions expose data but don't create any gas-related vulnerability in the contracts themselves.
recommended_mitigations: While not a vulnerability, adding a maximum staker count cap would be reasonable defense-in-depth
Document that any future code must avoid iterating over _stakerList on-chain
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Search all provided source for any for/while loop referencing _stakerList, stakerCount(), or getStakerAddress in a loop. 2. Confirm zero results. 3. Verify all _stakerList access patterns are O(1). 4. Confirm economic barriers by checking baseStake enforcement in createNewAssertion.
uncertainties: Code outside the provided scope (admin logic, ValidatorUtils, external tooling) may contain iteration-based staker logic, but this cannot be verified from provided code
Future upgrades could introduce iteration, but that would be a new defect not present now
additional_testing_needed: Review ValidatorUtils contract (referenced in deployment.ts but not provided) for any staker iteration
Review any admin/governance contracts that may iterate over stakers
